questions.prompt: How does Swift's throws/try model differ from unchecked exceptions in languages like Java or C++?

options.text: Failure points are marked in the type system: a failing function is annotated throws, and every call must be prefixed with try, so you can't accidentally propagate a throw — you either handle it, mark your own function throws to pass it up, or convert it. Errors are as visible as return values.
Swift exceptions unwind the stack silently and can propagate through any function without annotation, exactly like unchecked exceptions.
Swift has no way to handle errors; throws simply crashes the program.
throws is a runtime-only feature with no effect on function signatures or the type system.

questions.explanation: The chapter's central design point: throwing is in the signature and try marks every failure point, so there's no silent propagation — errors are as visible as return values. (b) describes exactly the unchecked-exception behavior Swift deliberately avoids. (c) is false; do/catch handles errors. (d) is false; throws is part of the signature and type-checked.

questions.prompt: Why is modeling an error type as an enum with associated values (e.g. corrupted(reason:)) the idiomatic choice?

options.text: Errors are values (any type conforming to the error protocol), so an enum lets callers pattern-match specific cases in catch and carries diagnostic data (like a reason) without stringly-typed hacks.
Enums are the only type in Swift allowed to conform to the error protocol; classes and structs cannot be errors.
Because enum errors are automatically logged by the runtime, unlike other error types.
Because associated values make the error crash instead of propagate, which is safer.

questions.explanation: The chapter: errors are values, so an enum with associated values enables precise case matching in catch and carries diagnostic data cleanly. (b) is false; structs and classes can also conform to the error protocol. (c) fabricates automatic logging. (d) is false; associated values carry data, they don't cause crashes.

questions.prompt: A developer replaces a do/catch with 'let config = try? loadConfig(at: path)' to simplify the code. loadConfig can throw either notFound or corrupted(reason:). What is lost?

options.text: try? collapses any failure into nil, discarding WHY it failed — you can no longer tell notFound from corrupted, which often matters; it's the right tool only when any failure genuinely collapses to 'no value' and the reason truly doesn't matter.
Nothing is lost; try? preserves the specific error in a hidden property you can inspect later.
try? makes the call crash on any error, so the risk is a crash rather than lost information.
try? changes the return type to a non-optional Config, removing the ability to represent absence.

questions.explanation: The chapter warns that try? throws away the error reason (success→value, failure→nil), so notFound vs corrupted becomes indistinguishable — acceptable only when the reason truly doesn't matter. (b) is false; try? discards the error entirely, it isn't stashed anywhere. (c) confuses try? (→ nil) with try! (→ crash). (d) reverses it — try? produces an optional, not a non-optional.

questions.prompt: When is Result the right tool rather than throws, especially given async/await now exists?

options.text: When a failure is a VALUE you carry around — stored, deferred, or passed through (retry queues, caching an outcome, batch results where each item independently succeeded or failed); async/await removed most of Result's old use as an async-callback carrier, so its remaining niche is outcomes-as-data.
Result should always be preferred over throws because it is strictly more powerful.
Result is now obsolete and should never be used since async/await exists.
Result is only for synchronous code and throws is only for asynchronous code.

questions.explanation: The chapter: Result fits when the outcome is data you store/defer/pass (retry queues, caching, per-item batch results); async/await removed its main old async use, but the outcomes-as-data niche remains. (b) overstates it — throws reads better for straight-line synchronous flow. (c) overcorrects into obsolescence; the data-carrying niche persists. (d) reverses the actual relationship (async functions can just be throws).

questions.prompt: The language team recommends defaulting to untyped throws for most APIs rather than typed throws. Why?

options.text: Committing to a concrete error type is a versioning constraint: adding a new error case becomes a breaking change to the API's contract; typed throws is valuable in narrow cases (exhaustive catch with no catch-all, embedded code avoiding existential boxing) but the general default stays untyped.
Typed throws is slower at runtime than untyped throws in every case.
Typed throws is deprecated and will be removed, so untyped is the only safe choice.
Untyped throws is required because Swift cannot represent a specific error type in a signature.

questions.explanation: The chapter's precise reason: a concrete error type is a versioning commitment (new error case = breaking change), so untyped is the default while typed throws serves narrow cases (exhaustive catch, embedded/no-boxing). (b) fabricates a blanket performance claim. (c) is false; typed throws is a real, supported feature. (d) is false; typed throws exists precisely because Swift CAN name the error type.

questions.prompt: Per the chapter's trade-off, how granular should an error type be?

options.text: Match the granularity to how differently callers actually need to REACT — rich enum error types are more precise but more to maintain, while a single catch-all tells callers nothing; if every failure is handled the same way, an elaborate error taxonomy is wasted effort.
Always maximally granular — every conceivable failure should be its own distinct case, regardless of how callers handle them.
Always a single generic error, because callers never need to distinguish failures.
Granularity should be decided by the number of functions that throw, not by caller behavior.

questions.explanation: The chapter's trade-off: granularity should track how differently callers react — precise enums cost maintenance, a catch-all conveys nothing, and identical handling makes fine taxonomies wasted. (b) and (c) are the two opposite overcorrections. (d) picks the wrong driver — it's caller reaction, not the count of throwing functions.
